subject: Economics
contextNote: JC1 Economics, 24 students. You modelled exchange rate determination — showing how a rise in oil prices increases import demand for USD, shifting the demand for USD right and causing the SGD to depreciate. Students are now 24 minutes into a set of exchange rate factor questions. Most have correct appreciation/depreciation answers, but the room is silent and no one is talking about the mechanism.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: All factors and directions correct. Sitting quietly, pen down. Has not explained the mechanism — just the direction of change.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: Correct appreciation/depreciation answers, head down. Has not written the mechanism chain — just the final direction.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Correct answers but working slowly. Has written the factor and the direction but not the intermediate steps in the mechanism.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Wrong direction for the oil price question — has written 'SGD appreciates' when it should depreciate. Has not written any mechanism.

ep1.situation: 24 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the silence is striking — not the productive hum of focused work, but the flat quiet of compliance without engagement. Rajan has all factors and directions correct with only the final answers written — 'SGD depreciates', 'SGD appreciates' — no mechanism chain, no explanation of why the exchange rate moves in that direction. He sits quietly, pen down, not asking for more work. Nur has correct answers as well, head down, not looking up. She has written the factor and the direction but has not explained the mechanism. Wei Jie has correct answers but is working slowly — he has written the factor and the direction but not the intermediate steps: why the demand for SGD changes, and how that affects the exchange rate. Siti has written 'SGD appreciates' for the oil price question — the wrong direction. She has not written any mechanism. A quick scan of the class: 8 students have all correct answers with no mechanism shown. 10 students have correct answers with partial working. 6 students, including Siti, have direction errors. The room is silent and compliant — but you cannot tell whether the silence masks understanding or confusion, and no one is talking about the mechanism. You have about 16 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: The silence reveals a participation gap, not just a readiness gap. Most students can identify the direction of exchange rate change, but no one is talking about the mechanism — why the demand or supply of the currency changes, and how that affects the exchange rate. Siti's direction error suggests she has not understood the mechanism at all.

choices.label: Thumbometer and two-step mechanism check
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and ask for a quick confidence check using thumbs: thumb up if you feel confident about the exchange rate mechanism, thumb sideways if you are unsure, thumb down if you are stuck. Then project a diagnostic question: 'A rise in oil prices causes the SGD to ___.' Ask two questions: '(1) What is the direction — appreciate or depreciate?' Then: '(2) Why? Choose: (a) Singapore imports oil in USD — demand for USD rises, demand for SGD falls, SGD depreciates. (b) Oil prices rise, so Singapore exports more, SGD appreciates. (c) The SGD always depreciates when oil prices rise. (d) I am not sure.' Ask students to write both answers on paper. Use the pattern to see who understands the mechanism and who is getting correct answers without the chain.
choices.tradeoff: The thumbometer gives you a quick, low-stakes gauge of confidence without requiring students to speak. The two-step diagnostic reveals whether students understand the mechanism or are getting correct answers by memory. But the thumbometer may not be honest — quiet students may put thumbs sideways even if they understand, to avoid standing out. The diagnostic interrupts the few students who are working productively.

choices.label: Paired mechanism chains with ZPD prompts
choices.elaboration: Assign shoulder partners and ask them to work through the next question together. Give the instruction: 'Before writing the direction, explain the mechanism: Factor → ___ because ___ → demand/supply of SGD ___ because ___ → SGD ___.' Distribute prompt cards to students who are stalled: 'Step 1: Identify the factor. Step 2: Does this increase or decrease demand for SGD? Why? Step 3: Does this increase or decrease supply of SGD? Why? Step 4: What happens to the exchange rate?' Circulate and listen for the quality of mechanism explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one or two pairs to share their chain with the class.
choices.tradeoff: Paired mechanism work breaks the silence in a low-risk way and gives students a structured approach to building the chain. The prompt cards support students with direction errors respectfully. But pair quality is uneven — if both students in a pair are silent or both have the same error, the pair will not be productive. Siti may copy her partner's mechanism without engaging with the reasoning.

choices.label: Accountable Talk: explain the appreciation/depreciation mechanism
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and introduce Accountable Talk stems. Write on the board: 'I claim the SGD ___ because ___. The mechanism is ___ because ___.' Ask students to turn to their shoulder partner and use the stem to explain their answer to the last question. The listener must respond: 'I agree because ___' or 'I disagree because ___.' Circulate and listen for the most common mechanism patterns and errors. After 3 minutes, select two contrasting pairs — one with clear mechanism reasoning, one with a direction error or shallow claim — and run a brief IRF probe: 'What did you notice about these two explanations? Which mechanism is stronger? Why?'
choices.tradeoff: Accountable Talk stems force students to make claims and justify them, not just share answers. The structure ensures mechanism reasoning is made explicit and breaks the silence in a structured way. But the stems may feel artificial or intimidating to quiet students. Siti may mumble the stem without genuine reasoning. Some students may say 'I agree' without actually evaluating their partner's mechanism.

A.consequence: The thumbs go up and the pattern is revealing. 8 students show thumbs up (confident), 12 show thumbs sideways (unsure), and 4 show thumbs down (stuck), including Siti. The thumbometer confirms what you suspected — most students are unsure, not confident. Then the diagnostic responses come in. 15 of 24 students got the correct direction (SGD depreciates). But on the mechanism question, only 9 students chose option (a) — the correct demand-for-USD reasoning. 6 students chose option (c) — they have a memorised rule without the mechanism. Nur got the direction right but chose option (c) — she is applying a rule without understanding why. Rajan got both right and chose option (a) with a clear explanation. Wei Jie got the direction right but chose option (c) — he is not using the mechanism. Siti got the direction wrong and chose option (d). The evidence is now clear: most students are getting correct directions without the mechanism, and Siti has a direction error. 14 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS peer feedback on mechanism reasoning
choices.elaboration: Use the thumbometer evidence to form pairs: students who showed thumbs up (like Rajan) are paired with students who showed thumbs sideways (like Nur and Wei Jie). Ask the confident student to give AQS feedback on their partner's diagnostic response: Affirm what is correct ('You got the right direction'), Question the mechanism ('You said the SGD always depreciates when oil prices rise — why? What is the mechanism?'), Suggest how to think about it ('Singapore imports oil in USD — demand for USD rises, demand for SGD falls, SGD depreciates'). Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their AQS exchange.
choices.tradeoff: AQS feedback gives quiet students structured practice in talking about the mechanism. The Affirm-Question-Suggest structure ensures feedback is specific and actionable. Pairing confident with unsure students leverages the expertise in the room. But the quality of peer explanation varies. Rajan may give minimal explanations because he is not used to articulating his reasoning. Siti, who has a direction error, needs her specific error addressed — not just a general prompt to explain the mechanism.

choices.label: IRF chains on mechanism reasoning
choices.elaboration: Select 3–4 students who got both diagnostic questions right and can explain the mechanism. Run an IRF chain with the whole class: 'Why does a rise in oil prices cause the SGD to depreciate? What is the mechanism?' Take the first student's response (Initiation). Then ask a second student: 'Can you add to what [first student] said?' (Response). Then ask a third student: 'Do you agree? Is there anything you would clarify or correct?' (Feedback). Build a complete, class-constructed explanation. Write the final mechanism on the board and ask all students to copy it into their notes.
choices.tradeoff: IRF chains build a shared explanation that is richer than any single student would produce. It makes the mechanism visible and public. But the chain is driven by the students who are already confident and can articulate their thinking. The majority of quiet students remain passive observers. Siti, who has a direction error, may copy the board explanation without correcting her underlying error.

B.consequence: The pairs begin working and the room shifts from silence to low murmurs. Rajan explains the mechanism to his partner quietly: 'Oil prices rise → Singapore imports more oil → demand for USD rises → demand for SGD falls → SGD depreciates.' His partner nods and writes it down. Nur is paired with another student who is also getting correct answers without the mechanism. They work through the chain together: 'Oil prices rise → SGD depreciates.' 'I agree.' But neither student explains why — they are confirming the direction without the mechanism. Wei Jie uses the prompt card and works through the steps carefully: 'Factor: oil prices rise. Demand for SGD: falls, because Singapore needs USD to pay for oil. Exchange rate: SGD depreciates.' He looks pleased. Siti is paired with a student who has the correct direction. Her partner explains: 'Oil prices rise → demand for USD rises → SGD depreciates.' Siti watches and copies the chain. But when you ask her 'Why does demand for USD rise?', she says 'Because my partner said so.' The prompt card is on her desk but she has not read it. 15 minutes remain.

choices.label: Flex grouping: conceptual pairs share with procedural pairs
choices.elaboration: Identify the 11 pairs who were reasoning about the mechanism clearly. Regroup the class: each reasoning pair joins with one procedural pair to form a group of 4. Ask the reasoning pair to explain their mechanism chain to the procedural pair. The procedural pair must ask at least one clarifying question: 'Why does demand for USD rise?' or 'How does a fall in demand for SGD cause depreciation?' Circulate and listen for whether the explanations deepen the procedural pairs' understanding.
choices.tradeoff: Flex grouping extends the ZPD scaffolding by pairing students who can reason about the mechanism with students who are still procedural. It gives the reasoning pairs a cognitively demanding task — teaching is harder than doing. But the procedural pairs may ask surface questions or just copy the mechanism without engaging with the reasoning. Siti, who is still copying without understanding, may be overwhelmed in a group of 4 and remain passive.

choices.label: Solo mechanism check: factor, chain, direction
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair work and ask all students to work individually. Write on the board: 'For the next question: (1) Identify the factor. (2) Write the mechanism chain: Factor → ___ because ___ → demand/supply of SGD ___ because ___ → SGD ___. (3) Write the direction.' Give 3 minutes, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who has genuine understanding of the mechanism from the pair work and who was just borrowing their partner's reasoning.
choices.tradeoff: Moving from pair work to solo mechanism work gives you individual evidence of understanding. It reveals whether the pair work built genuine learning or just social agreement. But it kills the emerging talk momentum in the room. Siti, who did not engage during pair work, will likely produce a blank or copied mechanism — revealing that the pair work did not help her understand the chain.

C.consequence: The Accountable Talk stems go up on the board and pairs begin using them. The room gets slightly louder — not noisy, but noticeably less silent than before. Rajan uses the stem with his partner: 'I claim the SGD depreciates because Singapore imports oil in USD — demand for USD rises, demand for SGD falls, SGD depreciates.' His partner responds: 'I agree because that matches what I got.' The exchange is correct but feels scripted. Nur is paired with another student who also got the direction right. They use the stems: 'I claim the SGD depreciates because oil prices rise.' 'I agree because that is what I got.' The stems are being used, but the mechanism is absent — they are confirming the direction, not explaining the chain. Wei Jie is paired with a student who has the same uncertainty about the mechanism. They use the stems: 'I claim the SGD depreciates because...' Wei Jie's partner pauses, then says 'because oil prices rise.' Neither student explains the demand-for-USD mechanism. Siti mumbles 'I agree' but does not offer her own claim. You circulate and hear the pattern: 7 pairs are using the stems to make genuine claims with mechanism reasoning. 11 pairs are using the stems but staying shallow — confirming directions without explaining the chain. 6 pairs are stuck in near-silence or echoing. 14 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS gallery critique on mechanism claims
choices.elaboration: Ask each pair to write their Accountable Talk claim on a sticky note: 'I claim the SGD ___ because ___. The mechanism is ___ because ___.' Post four notes on the board: one with clear mechanism reasoning, one with direction-only ('because oil prices rise'), one with a direction error, one with no mechanism. Run a whole-class AQS session on the direction-only note: Affirm what they attempted ('They used the stem structure and got the right direction'), Question what is missing ('Why does the SGD depreciate? What is the mechanism?'), Suggest how to deepen it ('Try adding: Singapore imports oil in USD — demand for USD rises, demand for SGD falls, SGD depreciates'). Ask all pairs to revise their sticky notes.
choices.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique makes the difference between direction-confirmation and mechanism reasoning visible to the whole class. It gives the shallow pairs a concrete model of what genuine mechanism reasoning looks like. But the pairs whose notes are displayed may feel exposed. Siti's pair will likely produce a shallow or incorrect note, and the AQS feedback may feel abstract to students who have not yet articulated the mechanism.

choices.label: Whole-class reteach: mechanism chain with diagram
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair work and address the shallow mechanism reasoning directly with the whole class. Draw the mechanism chain on the board: 'Oil prices rise → Singapore imports more oil → demand for USD rises → supply of SGD rises (Singaporeans sell SGD to buy USD) → SGD depreciates.' Emphasise: 'The mechanism is the chain of cause and effect — not just the direction, but why the exchange rate moves.' Ask all students to re-attempt the last question using the mechanism chain and write one sentence explaining each step.
choices.tradeoff: The whole-class reteach with mechanism chain is efficient and will reach the 11 pairs who were confirming directions without explaining the chain. It directly addresses Siti's direction error by showing the correct mechanism. But it shifts the room back to silence and teacher-led instruction. The students who were starting to reason aloud in pairs lose that momentum. Rajan, who already understands, is under-challenged.

A1.outcome: You pair Rajan with Nur and ask Rajan to give AQS feedback on Nur's diagnostic response. Rajan looks at Nur's paper and begins quietly: 'You got the right direction — SGD depreciates is correct. But you said the SGD always depreciates when oil prices rise. Why? What is the mechanism?' Nur draws the chain: 'Oil prices rise → demand for USD rises → demand for SGD falls → SGD depreciates.' Rajan nods: 'Good — and why does demand for USD rise?' Nur pauses, then says: 'Because Singapore imports oil in USD.' Rajan confirms. Wei Jie is paired with a student who chose option (a) and can explain the mechanism. His partner gives AQS feedback: 'You got the right direction. But you said the SGD always depreciates — try explaining the mechanism.' Wei Jie writes the chain with his partner's guidance. Siti is paired with a student who got both questions right. Her partner gives AQS feedback: 'Your direction is wrong — the SGD depreciates, not appreciates. Singapore imports oil in USD, so demand for USD rises and SGD falls.' Siti re-draws the chain and gets the correct direction. But when you ask her 'Why does demand for USD rise?', she says 'Because my partner said so.' On a follow-up check, 17 of 20 unsure students can now explain the mechanism, up from 9. The AQS feedback has activated more talk and improved mechanism reasoning for the middle group. But Siti is still dependent on copying, and Rajan is under-challenged. 11 minutes remain.
A1.tradeoff: AQS feedback gives quiet students structured practice in articulating mechanism reasoning. The Affirm-Question-Suggest structure ensures feedback is specific and actionable. Nur and Wei Jie made clear gains through peer support. But Siti's partner corrected her direction error without building genuine understanding — Siti can now produce the correct direction but cannot explain the mechanism independently. Rajan is giving rather than receiving.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose AQS peer feedback over whole-class discussion? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Nur and Wei Jie made gains, but Siti is still copying and Rajan is under-challenged. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive peer feedback in quiet classes?

How do you build a classroom culture where students speak with confidence about economic mechanisms — not just compliance?

A2.outcome: You select Rajan and two other students who got both diagnostic questions right and can explain the mechanism. You ask the whole class: 'Why does a rise in oil prices cause the SGD to depreciate? What is the mechanism?' Rajan responds first: 'Singapore imports oil in USD — when oil prices rise, Singapore needs more USD to pay for the same amount of oil. Demand for USD rises. To buy USD, Singaporeans sell SGD — supply of SGD rises. SGD depreciates.' You ask a second student to build on this: 'So the mechanism is: oil prices rise → import costs rise → demand for USD rises → supply of SGD rises → SGD depreciates.' A third student evaluates: 'Yes — and you can check by asking: does this factor increase or decrease demand for SGD? If demand for SGD falls, SGD depreciates.' The IRF chain produces a complete, detailed explanation. You write the final mechanism on the board and ask all students to copy it into their notes. Nur listens carefully and writes the mechanism down. Wei Jie copies the board notes. Siti copies the board notes mechanically. When you ask her 'Does this make sense?', she nods quickly but does not speak. Her next answer still has the wrong direction — she has copied the mechanism but not corrected her underlying error. The IRF chain has made the reasoning visible and public, but it is driven by the 3 students who spoke. The other 21 students are passive observers. 11 minutes remain.
A2.tradeoff: The IRF chain builds a shared, class-constructed explanation that is richer and more detailed than any single student would produce. It makes the mechanism visible and models what good justification looks like. Nur and Wei Jie heard a clear explanation. But the chain is driven by the students who are already confident. The majority of quiet students remain passive observers. Siti copied the mechanism but did not correct her direction error.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose IRF chains over peer feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The mechanism is now visible, but only 3 students spoke and Siti is still making direction errors. What does this tell you about who does the thinking in an IRF chain?

How do you design whole-class discussion so that quiet students participate and correct their errors, not just observe and copy?

B1.outcome: You regroup the class into groups of 4: each reasoning pair joins with one procedural pair. You ask the reasoning pairs to explain their mechanism chain to the procedural pairs. The procedural pairs must ask at least one clarifying question. Rajan's group begins. Rajan explains: 'Oil prices rise → Singapore imports more oil → demand for USD rises → supply of SGD rises → SGD depreciates.' The procedural pair asks: 'Why does supply of SGD rise?' Rajan responds: 'Because Singaporeans sell SGD to buy USD — they need USD to pay for the oil.' The procedural pair writes this down. Nur's group works similarly — the reasoning pair explains, the procedural pair asks a clarifying question, and the explanation deepens. Wei Jie is in a group where the reasoning pair explains clearly, but Wei Jie's procedural pair asks surface questions: 'Is this right? Is that right?' without engaging with the mechanism. Siti is in a group of 4 but remains silent. When the reasoning pair explains, Siti nods but does not ask a question. When prompted by you, she says quietly: 'I don't know what to ask.' The group continues without her. On a follow-up check, 16 of 24 students can now explain the mechanism, up from 9. 11 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: Flex grouping extends the ZPD scaffolding by pairing students who can reason about the mechanism with students who are still procedural. It gives the reasoning pairs a cognitively demanding task — teaching is harder than doing. Some procedural pairs asked genuine clarifying questions and deepened their understanding. But the procedural pairs often dominated the groups by asking surface questions. Siti remained passive and did not engage with the mechanism.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose flex grouping over solo work? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Some groups reasoned deeply, but others stayed procedural and Siti remained passive. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive group work in quiet classes?

How do you ensure that all students in a group are actively reasoning about the mechanism — not just observing or copying?

B2.outcome: You stop the pair work and ask all students to work individually on the solo mechanism check. The room goes silent again. Rajan draws a clear mechanism chain with all four steps labelled and writes: 'Oil prices rise → Singapore imports more oil → demand for USD rises → supply of SGD rises → SGD depreciates. I know this is correct because the mechanism follows the demand and supply of SGD — when supply of SGD rises, the exchange rate falls.' His response shows deep understanding and metacognitive awareness. Nur draws the mechanism chain correctly and writes: 'Oil prices rise → demand for USD rises → supply of SGD rises → SGD depreciates. I know this is correct because I followed the chain step by step.' Her response shows she has internalised the mechanism from the pair work. Wei Jie draws the mechanism chain and labels the steps correctly. His explanation says: 'I used the prompt card steps.' He is still dependent on the prompt card rather than the conceptual reasoning. Siti draws the mechanism chain but writes 'SGD appreciates' at the end — her direction error persists. Her explanation is blank. The solo check has given you honest individual evidence. Rajan and Nur have genuine understanding; Wei Jie is dependent on the prompt; Siti still has the direction error. 11 minutes remain.
B2.tradeoff: Solo mechanism work gives you clean, individual evidence of understanding — uncontaminated by pair talk. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine understanding of the mechanism. But Wei Jie is still dependent on the prompt card, and Siti's direction error has not been corrected by the pair work. The move to solo work killed the emerging talk momentum. You have clear evidence of the gap — but limited time to address it.
B2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from pair talk to solo mechanism work? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine understanding, but Wei Jie is prompt-dependent and Siti still has the direction error. What does this tell you about when pair talk builds genuine understanding versus borrowed reasoning?

How do you correct a direction error like Siti's — where the student associates oil prices with appreciation — without making her feel singled out in a quiet class?

C1.outcome: Four sticky notes are posted on the board. You run the AQS session on the direction-only note: 'I claim the SGD depreciates because oil prices rise.' Several students affirm: 'They used the stem structure and got the right direction.' You write this on the board. Then you ask: 'What should we question?' Nur raises her hand tentatively: 'They didn't explain the mechanism. Why does the SGD depreciate?' You write: 'Question: Why does the SGD depreciate? What is the mechanism?' A third student suggests: 'Add: Singapore imports oil in USD — demand for USD rises, supply of SGD rises, SGD depreciates.' You write: 'Suggest: Explain the mechanism — demand for USD rises → supply of SGD rises → SGD depreciates.' The AQS feedback is now complete and visible. You ask all pairs to revise their sticky notes. Several shallow pairs add mechanism reasoning to their notes for the first time. Wei Jie's pair revises their note to include 'demand for USD rises.' Siti's pair produces a revised note that says 'SGD depreciates' — correct direction — but when you ask Siti to demonstrate on a new question, she writes 'SGD appreciates' again. The AQS process has corrected the surface claim but not the underlying direction error. 11 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique made the mechanism visible and gave the shallow pairs a concrete model of what genuine mechanism reasoning looks like. Several pairs added mechanism reasoning to their notes for the first time. But Siti's direction error is not corrected by the AQS process — she can now write 'SGD depreciates' on a sticky note but still writes 'appreciates' when she calculates. The AQS feedback served the middle group but did not address the most fundamental error in the room.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose to use the gallery talk output as the basis for AQS feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Several pairs added mechanism reasoning, but Siti's direction error remains uncorrected. What does this tell you about the limits of whole-class feedback when a student has a specific, persistent error?

How do you address a direction error like Siti's in a quiet class — where students are reluctant to speak and errors are hidden behind correct-looking answers?

C2.outcome: The room goes quiet as students write their individual mechanism proofs. Rajan writes a complete, labelled mechanism chain: 'Oil prices rise → Singapore imports more oil in USD → demand for USD rises → Singaporeans sell SGD to buy USD → supply of SGD rises → SGD depreciates. I know this is correct because the mechanism follows the demand and supply of SGD — when supply rises, the exchange rate falls.' His proof is complete and precise. Nur writes: 'Oil prices rise → demand for USD rises → supply of SGD rises → SGD depreciates. The mechanism is the chain of cause and effect.' Her proof is correct and clear — the pair talk has helped her articulate the mechanism. Wei Jie writes: 'Oil prices rise → demand for USD rises → SGD depreciates.' He stops — he cannot explain why demand for USD rising causes SGD to depreciate, or what happens to supply of SGD. His proof is incomplete. Siti writes: 'Oil prices rise → SGD appreciates.' Her direction error persists. The individual writing has given you the clearest picture of each student's understanding — from Rajan's complete proof to Siti's direction error. But the move to solo writing has revealed that the pair talk did not build the full mechanism for Wei Jie or correct Siti's direction error.
C2.tradeoff: Individual written proofs give you the clearest, most honest evidence of each student's understanding of the exchange rate mechanism. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine understanding. But the move to solo writing revealed that the pair talk did not build the full mechanism for Wei Jie or correct Siti's direction error. The pair talk produced surface connections rather than genuine mechanism understanding for most students.
C2.reflection:
Why did you move from pair talk to individual written proofs? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine understanding, but Wei Jie's proof is incomplete and Siti's direction error persists. What does this tell you about when pair talk builds genuine mechanism understanding versus surface-level direction confirmation?

How do you design a lesson that builds the exchange rate mechanism for all students — including those who are silent and those who have persistent direction errors?
